speaker: interviewer
text: Louis Stepak, 11/6/96. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So you said that you've experienced a lot. You've gone through a lot. Anything that you want to tell people who are watching this, a message or something you learned from the Holocaust? Something you want to tell them, anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] What can I tell?

speaker: interviewer
text: About your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the people know-- the people know the same thing what I know. I think so. They know it. They came-- they came-- what happened. We have to-- we have to-- I-- I don't know what to say, honest. I know this thing, that led to it, things like that, to get-- to kill people, live people. Live people killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: How can-- how can the war let do with that? Now they're talking the same thing. Some people-- some people saying there was not even was the Holocaust, some people. How they can say that? How does that make you feel when you hear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It make me feel very, very bad, very mean, very upset, very upset. When I hear-- when I see in the television something, Nazis coming on, like, Nazis just dressed, I get upset. Everything I get upset. It's unbelievable. It's unbelievable this can happen. It's unbelievable. Nobody-- it's unbelievable what this can happen. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it could happen again?

speaker: interviewee
text: God forbid. I would say no. God forbid, no. No, no way, no way. When we have that time maybe Israel maybe have, maybe a big help to us. First of all, they would take in all of us. They would take in. We don't have nobody to take us in. Come in America, they let the ships go back. And Hitler said, nobody wants you, and  they killed them. That's-- that's what we missed that time. I hope for it never happens, this. And if we happen, we know what to do. I think we know now what to do. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I think we have some pictures we'll take a look at. And maybe we'll invite your-- your wife in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eva. We'll ask Eva to come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eva!

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Louis, why don't we start with this picture on the left. Who are we seeing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the left?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this Eileen Stepak, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next, just go right through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next is mine lovely second wife. And next, that's I am. And next is Mike Stepak. And next is Asar Stepak.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the far right. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] We just need-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the picture on the right? That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Asar Stepak. Asar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Louis, could you just introduce your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this my wife, second wife, Eva Stepak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Stepak, we just finished the interview with Louis. And I know it's something that Mike wanted-- wanted him to get done. And he's glad-- glad that he's finished the interview now. Is there anything that you'd like to say about-- about it? I know you're-- you're from Kolki too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm from Kolki also. Well, we didn't know too much each other, but I did know his first wife. I-- I knew her. We were probably together till the fifth grade, and then she moved to another village. But we came back to America. We just got in contact. We knew each other, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've been married since?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- to my second-- it's been 14 years married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since 1982.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1982, yes. And his first wife died. My husband, also. It happened not too good. He was shot in a grocery, yeah. We knew each other. He called me. We got-- got together. We got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else you want to say or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing to say. What's to say? I think it-- we both-- well, just it happened. It's sorry. His wife passed away. So did my husband. But just we got together. And we just made it together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, thanks for-- for joining us. And Louis, thanks for sharing your experiences with us. I think the world can learn a lot from the experiences that-- that you went through, hopefully. Any final parting words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much, and I hope everything for now, we-- they'll never happen again, things like we went through. I hope everything be all right and the people understand that everybody got to live in-- in the right way, and no difference between people and people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Amen.
